name: Walnut Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Walnut. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for walnut surface cleaning without charring

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength for optimal absorption in walnut lignin and contaminants

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control on walnut surface

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without wood damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of surface contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for delicate wood cleaning to avoid thermal damage; starting at 50 W for light contamination on walnut, max 150 W before charring risk. Based on manufacturer specs from CleanLaser systems for organic materials and studies on wood ablation (e.g., Ancona et al., 2010, Optics Express).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contamination removal; 1500 mm/s recommended for optimal balance on walnut to prevent overheating. Lower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), higher for light (2000-3000 mm/s). From industry standards in laser cleaning manuals (e.g., IPG Photonics guidelines) and case studies on wood surface prep.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of walnut; 30 ns recommended to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ) due to walnut's low thermal conductivity (~0.17 W/mK). Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but less common industrially. Supported by laser physics research (e.g., Jaeschke et al., 2016, Journal of Laser Applications).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning; 40 kHz recommended for walnut to balance speed and heat accumulation—higher rates (>50 kHz) risk buildup on low-conductivity wood. Trade-off: faster cleaning but potential charring. From academic literature on wood laser processing (e.g., Pouli et al., 2015, Applied Physics A).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe ablation of contaminants; 0.5 J/cm² recommended for walnut to remove surface layers without damage (threshold ~1.5-2 J/cm² for charring at 1064 nm). Conservative due to walnut's moderate reflectivity (~40% at IR). From ablation threshold studies (e.g., Batani et al., 2006, Applied Surface Science).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter for controlled cleaning; 200 μm recommended for walnut to allow fine surface preparation without excessive heat spread. Smaller (100-200 μm) for detail, larger (300-500 μm) for broad areas. Per equipment specs from Adapt Laser Systems and industry standards for wood applications.

pass_count.description: Typically 1-3 passes for complete removal on wood; 2 passes recommended for walnut to ensure uniform cleaning of contaminants without substrate damage. Multi-pass for heavy soiling. Based on industry practice and case studies (e.g., laser cleaning of wooden artifacts, Vergés-Belmin, 2005, Studies in Conservation).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended for walnut to avoid gaps or overexposure, considering its density and low thermal diffusion. Typical 30-80% range from process optimization. From studies on scan strategies (e.g., Kumar et al., 2018, Optics and Lasers in Engineering).

material_characteristics.label: Material Characteristics
material_characteristics.description: Intrinsic physical, mechanical, chemical, and structural properties affecting cleaning outcomes and material integrity

laserDamageThreshold.source_name: Threshold fluences for laser cleaning of walnut wood
laserDamageThreshold.citation: Vazquez, E., et al., Journal of Laser Applications, Vol. 27, No. 2, 2015, DOI: 10.2351/1.4907852
laserDamageThreshold.context: Dry walnut wood (Juglans spp., 8% moisture content), 25°C, measured with Q-switched Nd:YAG laser at 1064 nm, 7 ns pulse duration, atmospheric pressure

laser_material_interaction.label: Laser-Material Interaction
laser_material_interaction.description: Optical and thermal properties governing laser energy absorption, reflection, propagation, and ablation thresholds

ablationThreshold.source_name: Laser Ablation Thresholds for Different Wood Species
ablationThreshold.citation: Panzner et al., Applied Physics A: Materials Science & Processing, 1998, DOI: 10.1007/s003390050456
ablationThreshold.context: Black walnut (Juglans nigra) heartwood, oven-dried (0% moisture), 25°C, measured with 1064 nm Nd:YAG nanosecond pulsed laser (10 ns pulse duration), perpendicular incidence
